# Bio-Monitor: Keeping an eye on astronauts' vital signs

![](/images/sciences/biomoniteur-ban.jpg)

The Bio-Monitor is a wearable technology designed to fit into an astronaut's daily routine aboard the [International Space Station](/eng/iss/) (ISS) while monitoring and recording vital signs.

This system, which includes a smart shirt, headband and dedicated tablet application, helps keep an eye on astronauts' health and enables new science by continuously measuring physiological data.

## Background

Doing science in space is no easy task. To participate in health experiments, astronauts must use several medical devices including electrocardiographs, blood pressure cuffs, fingertip oxygen saturation monitors, and ankle-bracelet activity sensors.

These devices are often bulky and invasive. Using them is disruptive and time-consuming.

The Bio-Monitor simplifies the process by combining numerous devices into one set of easy-to-use smart garments that records vital sign data. The system measures the following:

The garments can also be worn during sleep and exercise. The system is designed to easily send information to the ground, where scientists can monitor the astronauts' health around the clock as they orbit the planet.

Canadian Space Agency (CSA) astronaut David Saint-Jacques explains how the Bio-Monitor, a Canadian smart shirt system, measures and records astronauts' vital signs on the ISS. (Credits: CSA, NASA)

[Transcript](https://www.asc-csa.gc.ca/eng/multimedia/search/video/18316#transcript)

## Objectives

Using the Bio-Monitor on the ISS allows scientists to:

## Impacts on Earth

This system has the potential to help Canadians who are bedridden, housebound, or living in rural communities with limited access to medical support. It can also be worn by workers in dangerous environments such as mines, industrial sites, or factories.

The commercial version of the Bio-Monitor shirt, Hexoskin, is used by hundreds of institutional partners for research purposes, in studies examining breathing disorders, cardiac and pulmonary diseases, rare diseases, stress, sleep, epilepsy, and other conditions. It also offers wearable sensing solutions for pediatric research. The Hexoskin Junior biometric shirts are used by research groups to study sleep patterns, respiratory health, and rare diseases in children 3 to 17 years old.

## How it works

![David Saint-Jacques and Bio-Monitor](https://www.asc-csa.gc.ca/images/recherche/tiles/8968b9eb-3570-4af2-a61e-2e6392d00c43.jpg)

CSA astronaut David Saint-Jacques tries the Bio-Monitor, a new Canadian technology, for the first time in space. The innovative smart shirt system is designed to measure and record astronauts' vital signs. (Credit: CSA/NASA)

![Bio-Monitor: Canada's Smart Shirt for Space - Infographic](https://www.asc-csa.gc.ca/images/recherche/tiles/92e75d06-578a-49ee-b70d-5bc7fc8ca141.jpg)

[Bio-Monitor: Canada's Smart Shirt for Space - Infographic - Text version](https://www.asc-csa.gc.ca/eng/multimedia/search/image/8166#longdesc)

This infographic features an image of Bio-Monitor, a "smart shirt" worn by astronauts during their missions to the ISS. Credit: CSA

## Timeline

The Bio-Monitor system was launched into space in December 2018.

## Developers

Carré Technologies of Montreal, Quebec, developed Bio-Monitor for the CSA. Three additional Canadian companies lent their support:

# Wearable Tech for Space Station Research

![NASA astronaut and Expedition 70 Flight Engineer Jeanette Epps is pictured floating in microgravity aboard the International Space Station.](https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/53589381459-01243dd5d3-o-1.jpg?w=1536)

### Melissa L. Gaskill

#### International Space Station Research Communications Team

## Contents

## Science in Space Nov 2024

Many of us wear devices that count our steps, measure our heart rate, track sleep patterns, and more. This information can help us make healthy decisions – research shows the devices encourage people to move more, for example – and could flag possible problems, such as an irregular heartbeat.

Wearable monitors also have become common tools for research on human health, including studies on the International Space Station. Astronauts have worn special watches, headbands, vests, and other devices to help scientists examine sleep quality, effectiveness of exercise, heart health, and more.

## Warm to the core

Spaceflight can affect body temperature regulation and daily rhythms due to factors such as the absence of convection (a natural process that transfers heat away from the body) and changes in the cardiovascular and metabolic systems.

A current investigation from ESA (European Space Agency), Thermo-Mini or [T-Mini](https://www.nasa.gov/mission/station/research-explorer/investigation/?#id=8999) examines how the body regulates its core temperature during spaceflight. The study uses a non-invasive headband monitor that astronauts can wear for hours at a time. Data from the monitor allow researchers to determine the effect on body temperature from environmental and physiological factors such as room temperature and humidity, time of day, and physical stress. The same type of sensor already is used on Earth for research in clinical environments, such as improving incubators, and studies of how hotter environments affect human health.

[Thermolab](https://www.nasa.gov/mission/station/research-explorer/investigation/?#id=297), an earlier ESA investigation, examined thermoregulatory and cardiovascular adaptations during rest and exercise in microgravity. Researchers [found](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-017-15560-w) that core body temperature rises higher and faster during exercise in space than on Earth and that the increase was sustained during rest, a phenomenon that could affect the health of crew members on long-term spaceflight. The finding also raises questions about the thermoregulatory set point humans are assumed to have as well as our ability to adapt to climate change on Earth.

![Hague wears a black t-shirt and the blue T-mini headband, which has a small control panel. He is holding a white towel in his left hand and smiling.](https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/iss072e031342.jpg?w=1365)

## To sleep, perchance to dream

Spaceflight is known to disrupt sleep-wake patterns. [Actiwatch Spectrum](https://www.nasa.gov/mission/station/research-explorer/facility/?#id=838), a device worn on the wrist, contains an accelerometer to measure motion and photodetectors to monitor ambient lighting. It is an upgrade of previous technology used on the space station to monitor the length and quality of crew member sleep. Data from earlier missions [show](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S147444221470122X?via%3Dihub)that crew members slept significantly less during spaceflight than before and after. The [Actiwatch Sleep-Long](https://www.nasa.gov/mission/station/research-explorer/investigation/?#id=286) investigation used an earlier version of the device to examine how ambient light affects the sleep-wake cycle and [found](https://www.nature.com/articles/npjmgrav201519) an association between sleep deficiency and changes during spaceflight in circadian patterns, or the body’s response to a normal 24-hour light and dark cycle. Follow up studies are testing lighting systems to address these effects and help astronauts maintain healthy circadian rhythms.

![Williams is wearing a blue t-shirt, her hair floating above her head. She is adjusting a piece of equipment on a worktable with her right hand and holding a sample container in her left. She wears a black Actiwatch around her left wrist.](https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/iss014e12135.jpg?w=2048)

[Wearable Monitoring](https://www.nasa.gov/mission/station/research-explorer/investigation/?#id=1144) tested a lightweight vest with embedded sensors to monitor heart rate and breathing patterns during sleep and help determine whether changes in heart activity affect sleep quality. The technology offers a significant advantage by monitoring heart activity without waking the test subject and could help patients on Earth with sleep disorders. Researchers [reported](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0924424717321969?via%3Dihub) positive performance and good quality of recorded signals, suggesting that the vest can contribute to comprehensive monitoring of individual health on future spaceflight and in some settings on Earth as well.

These and other studies support development of countermeasures to improve sleep for crew members, helping to maintain alertness and lessen fatigue during missions.

## (Not) waiting to exhale

Humans exhale carbon dioxide and too much of it can build up in closed environments, causing headaches, dizziness, and other symptoms. Spacecraft have systems to remove this substance from cabin air, but pockets of carbon dioxide can form and be difficult to detect and remove. [Personal CO2 Monitor](https://www.nasa.gov/mission/station/research-explorer/investigation/?#id=1839) tested specially designed sensors attached to clothing to monitor the wearer’s immediate surroundings. Researchers [reported](https://ttu-ir.tdl.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/72ada33c-f401-4579-9b1d-41ad915833ab/content) that the devices functioned adequately as either crew-worn or static monitors, an important step toward using them to determine how carbon dioxide behaves in enclosed systems like spacecraft.

![A black rectangular device the size of a computer mouse with a round window in its center is clipped onto a black cord hanging from the edge of the white material that covers crew sleeping quarters.](https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/iss049e004440.jpg?w=2048)

## Radiation in real time

[EVARM](https://www.nasa.gov/mission/station/research-explorer/investigation/?#id=162), an investigation from CSA (Canadian Space Agency), used small wireless dosimeters carried in a pocket to measure radiation exposure during spacewalks. The data [showed](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/4493407) that this method is a feasible way to measure radiation exposure, which could help focus routine dosage monitoring where it is most needed. Any shielding and countermeasures developed also could help protect people who work in high-radiation areas on Earth.

ESA’s [Active Dosimeter](https://www.nasa.gov/mission/station/research-explorer/investigation/?#id=1869) tested a radiation dosimeter worn by crew members to measure changes in their exposure over time based on the space station’s orbit and altitude, the solar cycle, and solar flares. Measurements from the device [allowed](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0939388923000387?via%3Dihub) researchers to analyze radiation dosage across an entire space mission.

![Pesquet, wearing a red polo shirt and green pants, holds a small, black rectangular device the size of a pack of cards. The device has a large white label on the front and on its end, a gold sensor strip and a blue light.](https://images-assets.nasa.gov/image/iss051e051544/iss051e051544~large.jpg?w=1920&h=1277&fit=clip&crop=faces%2Cfocalpoint)

The Active Dosimeter also was among the instruments used to [measure radiation](https://www.nasa.gov/missions/artemis/artemis-1/artemis-i-radiation-measurements-validate-orion-safety-for-astronauts/) on NASA’s Orion spacecraft during its 25.5-day uncrewed Artemis I mission around the Moon and back in 2022.

Another device tested on the space station and then on Artemis I, [AstroRad Vest](https://www.nasa.gov/mission/station/research-explorer/investigation/?#id=7803) is designed to protect astronauts from solar particle events. Researchers used these and other radiation measuring devices to show that Orion’s design can protect its crew from potentially hazardous radiation levels during lunar missions.

The International Space Station serves as an important testbed for these technologies and many others being developed for future missions to the Moon and beyond.

***Melissa Gaskill*** *****International Space Station Research Communications Team*****  
***Johnson Space Center***

enriched_points.expanded_content: Innovative devices like the Bio-Monitor allow astronauts to continuously track vital signs, facilitating real-time health assessments during missions aboard the ISS.
Systems enable constant transmission of health data to ground control, allowing medical experts to monitor astronauts' conditions and respond promptly to any health issues.
Advanced algorithms analyze collected health data, identifying potential risks and enabling proactive measures to enhance astronaut safety during space missions.
Wearable health technologies are seamlessly integrated with mission protocols, ensuring compliance with health standards and facilitating optimal performance in space environments.
